title: Meta Statements And Geography
description: Cross-cutting meta-statements about Commonality plus geographic hierarchy and example intersection statements.
notes: These statements express the system's own thesis and provide top-level parent statements for many implication links.
The geographic hierarchy is intentionally plain-English rather than machine-semantic.

groups.title: Commonality thesis

statements.text: Public goods are chronically underfunded because the incentive structure is broken, not because people don't care.
People should be able to fund the things they care about without needing permission from the government or a large institution.
It should be possible to contribute to public goods without trusting a bureaucracy or a charity to spend your money wisely.
Transparency and accountability in how money is spent is more important than who controls the money.
Local problems are best solved by local people with local knowledge.
The ability to fund things collectively without a central coordinator is a technology worth building.
Neither the left nor the right has a monopoly on good ideas about how to organize society.

implicationNotes: "Local communities should fund their own priorities" implies both the permissionless-funding and local-knowledge meta statements.
"Every dollar of public spending should be auditable" implies the transparency-over-control statement.
"Censorship-resistant publishing infrastructure" implies "Decentralization of power is generally better than centralization".
"Open-source software deserves sustained funding" implies "Open-source public infrastructure benefits everyone".
"Open-access publishing" implies "Scientific research funded by the public should be freely accessible".
The political-tribalism hidden-majority commonality statement implies the anti-tribalism meta statement.

groups.title: Canadian geography
notes: Geographic parent statements to support geographic x topical intersections.

statements.text: I care about improving Canada
I care about improving Ontario
I care about improving Quebec
I care about improving British Columbia
I care about improving Alberta
I care about improving Nova Scotia

implicationNotes: "I care about improving Ontario" implies "I care about improving Canada".

groups.title: United States geography

statements.text: I care about improving the United States
I care about improving California
I care about improving Texas
I care about improving New York
I care about improving Florida

implicationNotes: "I care about improving California" implies "I care about improving the United States".

groups.title: Example intersections
notes: These are examples of the geographic x topical conjunction pattern.

statements.text: I'm interested in crypto in Ontario
notes: Implies both "I care about crypto" and "I care about improving Ontario".

statements.text: I'm interested in open-source civic tools for Ontario municipalities
I'm interested in local community resilience in Ontario

implicationNotes: Conjunction statements like these should be modeled as ordinary statements with direct implication links to their topical and geographic parents.
Because implications are non-transitive, useful geographic rollups need direct edges rather than chains.
